quiz.name: Session 04 Final Quiz — Fundamentals of Cryptography
quiz.description: Post-session assessment of cryptography concepts. 7 MCQ, 15 minutes, passing score 5/7.

questiontext.text: A developer stores encrypted database records using AES-128-ECB mode. An attacker gains read access to the encrypted database. Which statement is true?

answer.text: The attacker cannot learn anything because AES-128 is unbreakable by brute force

feedback.text: Incorrect. While AES-128 is indeed brute-force resistant, ECB mode has a structural weakness that reveals information WITHOUT needing to break AES at all.

answer.text: The attacker can identify records that contain the same plaintext in the same position, even without the key

feedback.text: Correct! ECB encrypts each 16-byte block independently with the same key. Identical plaintext blocks → identical ciphertext blocks. An attacker can detect which records share the same data (e.g., same account balance) without ever breaking AES. This is why ECB must NEVER be used.

answer.text: ECB is actually stronger than CBC because it has no IV dependency

feedback.text: Incorrect. The absence of an IV is a weakness, not a strength. CBC's IV prevents identical plaintext blocks from producing identical ciphertext blocks.

answer.text: The attacker must break SHA-256 first before exploiting ECB mode

feedback.text: Incorrect. The ECB weakness is entirely independent of hash functions. It exploits the deterministic block-by-block encryption, not any hash property.

questiontext.text: A security team discovers that the corporate VPN has PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy) disabled. The VPN server's RSA private key is later stolen in a breach. What is the security impact compared to a PFS-enabled VPN?

answer.text: No difference; RSA private key theft only affects future sessions

feedback.text: Incorrect. Without PFS, the RSA private key can decrypt past sessions too. This is exactly the risk PFS is designed to prevent.

answer.text: Only sessions established after the theft are compromised

feedback.text: Incorrect. That would be the case with PFS enabled. Without PFS, historical sessions are also vulnerable.

answer.text: ALL previously recorded VPN sessions can now be decrypted by anyone who recorded the traffic

feedback.text: Correct! Without PFS, the session key is protected by the long-term RSA key. Anyone who recorded past encrypted traffic can now decrypt it using the stolen RSA key. With PFS (ephemeral ECDHE), each session generates unique keys that are immediately discarded, so past sessions remain secure even if the long-term key is compromised.

answer.text: Only the authentication is affected; session data remains encrypted

feedback.text: Incorrect. The RSA key protects both authentication AND the key exchange that establishes the session encryption key. Stealing the RSA key allows recovering the session keys.

questiontext.text:
A SIEM rule triggers on outbound HTTPS connections from a workstation with these characteristics:

  • Connection interval: exactly 60 seconds
  • Certificate: issued 1 day ago
  • Domain: registered 3 days ago

What is the MOST likely explanation?

answer.text: A scheduled Windows Update check

feedback.text: Incorrect. Windows Update connects to known Microsoft domains with long-established certificates, not newly-registered domains with brand-new certificates. Also, WU doesn't beacon at precisely 60-second intervals.

answer.text: A legitimate cloud backup service with a new certificate

feedback.text: Incorrect. A legitimate service would use an established domain (months/years old), not one registered 3 days ago. The combination of all three indicators is highly suspicious.

answer.text: A malware C2 (command-and-control) beacon

feedback.text: Correct! The three indicators together are classic C2 beaconing: (1) exact 60s interval = Cobalt Strike default beacon sleep, (2) 1-day-old certificate = fresh malware infrastructure, (3) 3-day-old domain = newly registered for attack campaign. Legitimate software doesn't exhibit all three simultaneously.

answer.text: A misconfigured network monitoring agent

feedback.text: Incorrect. A legitimate monitoring agent would use the organization's own domain/certificate infrastructure, not a brand-new external domain with a fresh certificate.

questiontext.text: Which describes the CORRECT approach to password storage?

answer.text: Hash passwords with SHA-256 and store the hash; compare hashes on login

feedback.text: Incorrect. SHA-256 is designed to be FAST (billions/second on GPU). Without a salt, identical passwords produce identical hashes (rainbow tables work). Slow password-specific KDFs (bcrypt, Argon2) are required.

answer.text: Encrypt passwords with AES-256 and store the ciphertext

feedback.text: Incorrect. Encryption is reversible; if the AES key is compromised, all passwords are exposed in plaintext. Password hashing should be one-way — you should never need to "decrypt" a password.

answer.text: Use bcrypt or Argon2 with a unique random salt per user; store the hash with the salt

feedback.text: Correct! bcrypt and Argon2 are intentionally slow (configurable work factor), making brute-force impractical. Per-user random salts prevent rainbow table attacks. The salt is stored alongside the hash (it doesn't need to be secret). This is the industry standard.

answer.text: Hash passwords with MD5 and use a fixed salt applied to all passwords

feedback.text: Incorrect. MD5 is broken (fast and collision-prone). A fixed salt shared across all users is better than no salt but still allows dictionary attacks since an attacker only needs to crack one salt value for all users.

questiontext.text: During TLS analysis, you see the SNI field contains "secure-banking.paypa1.com" and the certificate was issued 2 hours ago by Let's Encrypt. What should you conclude?

answer.text: This is normal — Let's Encrypt issues certificates quickly and all HTTPS is equally secure

feedback.text: Incorrect. HTTPS (TLS) provides transport encryption but does NOT authenticate that a site is legitimate. Let's Encrypt only verifies domain control, not business identity. A criminal can get a Let's Encrypt cert for a phishing domain in minutes.

answer.text: This is likely a phishing site: the domain is a typosquat of "paypal.com" with a very fresh certificate

feedback.text: Correct! "paypa1.com" (number 1 instead of letter l) is a classic typosquat of paypal.com. A 2-hour-old Let's Encrypt certificate means fresh, quickly-spun-up infrastructure — typical of phishing campaigns. SOC should block this domain and alert users.

answer.text: The connection is safe because Let's Encrypt only issues certificates to legitimate businesses

feedback.text: Incorrect. Let's Encrypt issues Domain Validated (DV) certificates to anyone who can prove control of the domain — no business identity verification is required. Phishing sites routinely use Let's Encrypt certificates.

answer.text: The "1" instead of "l" is insignificant since browsers handle IDN normalization

feedback.text: Incorrect. "paypa1.com" uses an ASCII digit 1, not an IDN/Unicode lookalike. Browsers do NOT normalize this — it looks nearly identical to "paypal.com" to users but is a completely different domain. This is a significant phishing indicator.

questiontext.text: An organization's critical data (25-year retention requirement) is protected with RSA-2048 encryption. In the context of post-quantum cryptography, what is the most accurate risk assessment?

answer.text: No risk — RSA-2048 provides 112-bit security which is sufficient for any foreseeable threat

feedback.text: Incorrect. RSA-2048's 112-bit classical security is irrelevant against Shor's algorithm, which solves the integer factorization problem in polynomial time on a quantum computer. RSA-2048 provides ZERO quantum security.

answer.text: The data is at risk from "harvest now, decrypt later" attacks — adversaries recording encrypted data today could decrypt it with quantum computers before the 25-year retention ends

feedback.text: Correct! "Harvest now, decrypt later": adversaries record encrypted data today, then decrypt it when quantum computers become available (potentially within 5-15 years). Data retained until 2049 (25 years from 2024) will still be sensitive long after quantum computers may exist. RSA-2048 is broken by Shor's algorithm regardless of key size.

answer.text: Only real-time attacks are relevant; stored encrypted data cannot be retroactively decrypted

feedback.text: Incorrect. This is precisely what "harvest now, decrypt later" attacks accomplish — storing encrypted data now to decrypt later when better tools (quantum computers) are available. AES-256 data stored long-term is safer; RSA-encrypted data is not.

answer.text: RSA-2048 is quantum-resistant because it uses a 2048-bit key which exceeds the 128-bit Grover threshold

feedback.text: Incorrect. Grover's algorithm (which threatens symmetric crypto like AES) is not the relevant attack against RSA. Shor's algorithm attacks RSA by solving integer factorization, and it completely breaks RSA regardless of key size. AES-256 is quantum-resistant; RSA is not.

questiontext.text: A file's SHA-256 hash is checked against a vendor-published checksum and they match. Which statement is most accurate?

answer.text: The file is definitely authentic and unmodified since the vendor released it

feedback.text: Incorrect. The hash proves the file content matches what was hashed when the checksum was created. But if an attacker controls the distribution server, they can modify both the file AND the checksum file.

answer.text: The file content matches the state when the checksum was computed, BUT authenticity depends on trusting the source of the checksum itself

feedback.text: Correct! A matching hash proves content integrity relative to the checksum. But if the checksum was tampered with (e.g., attacker controls the server), a matching hash tells you nothing about authenticity. Checksums should be GPG-signed and/or distributed via a separate trusted channel. Also: a clean hash doesn't mean a file is safe — only unmodified from when it was hashed.

answer.text: SHA-256 collision resistance guarantees authenticity regardless of checksum distribution

feedback.text: Incorrect. SHA-256 collision resistance means you can't find two different inputs with the same hash — it doesn't prevent an attacker from replacing BOTH the file AND the checksum with a consistent malicious pair.

answer.text: The file is safe to execute since SHA-256 is secure and cannot be forged

feedback.text: Incorrect. "Safe to execute" requires more than hash verification. The hash only proves the file wasn't modified from the hashed version. If the original vendor release itself contained malware, or if the checksum was replaced by an attacker, the file is not safe.
